original_abstract:
This study presents the detailed molecular characterization of atmospheric organic matter (AOM) in supercooled cloud water collected at the Storm Peak Laboratory in the Rocky Mountains. Using ultrahigh-resolution Fourier-transform ion cyclotron resonance (FT-ICR) mass spectrometry, approximately 3,000 unique molecular formulas were identified in each sample within a mass range of 100 to 700 Da. The identified species were categorized into four elemental groups: CHO, CHNO, CHOS, and CHNOS. Nitrogen-containing compounds (CHNO) were unexpectedly the most frequent and abundant species, likely influenced by residential wood combustion and nocturnal nitrate radical chemistry.

The overall organic matter displayed an average oxygen-to-carbon (O/C) ratio of ~0.6, suggesting a higher degree of oxidation compared to typical aerosol water-soluble organic carbon, which may point toward aqueous-phase processing within the clouds. Approximately 60% of the CHO formulas matched those found in laboratory-generated biogenic secondary organic aerosol (SOA) from monoterpene and sesquiterpene precursors. Additionally, the presence of both oxidized organosulfates and reduced sulfur species suggests a complex interplay between biogenic emissions and anthropogenic influences, such as coal-fired power plants and local combustion. These results provide significant insights into the chemical complexity of cloud water and the role of aqueous-phase reactions in transforming atmospheric organic aerosols.
humanized_abstract:
The detailed molecular-level analysis of individual atmospheric organic matter (AOM) molecules in supercooled cloud water collected at the Storm Peak Laboratory (SPL) in the Rocky Mountains is reported. Using ultrahigh-resolution Fourier-transform ion cyclotron resonance (FT-ICR) mass spectrometry, ca. 3000 unique molecular formulas were identified within a mass range of 100 to 700 Da in each sample. Based on the elemental composition of their molecular formulas, these species were assigned to one of four classes: CHO, CHNO, CHOS, and CHNOS. Surprisingly, species that contain nitrogen, classified as CHNO, are the most frequent and most abundant. Possible factors that could contribute to their observed high abundance include residential wood burning and reactions occurring during the nocturnal time period when nitrate radical reactions are possible.

The overall organic matter displayed an average oxygen-to-carbon (O/C) ratio of ~0.6, which is a higher degree of oxidation than that typically observed in aerosol water-soluble organic carbon (WSOC) and implies that some aqueous-phase processing must have occurred in clouds. About 60% of the CHO formulas observed fit into categories that can be attributed to lab-generated biogenic secondary organic aerosol (SOA) products of monoterpene and sesquiterpene oxidation. In addition, the presence of both oxidized and reduced organosulfates in addition to reduced sulfur compounds implies strong interaction between biogenic emissions and other sources including potential anthropogenic such as those from coal-fired power plants and local combustion sources. Overall, these findings significantly advance our knowledge of the chemical composition of cloud water and the role of aqueous-phase processes affecting atmospheric organic aerosols.

document.output:
The detailed molecular-level analysis of individual atmospheric organic matter (AOM) molecules in supercooled cloud water collected at the Storm Peak Laboratory (SPL) in the Rocky Mountains is reported. Using ultrahigh-resolution Fourier-transform ion cyclotron resonance (FT-ICR) mass spectrometry, ca. 3000 unique molecular formulas were identified within a mass range of 100 to 700 Da in each sample. The overall organic matter displayed an average oxygen-to-carbon (O/C) ratio of ~0.6, which is a higher degree of oxidation than that typically observed in aerosol water-soluble organic carbon (WSOC) and implies that some aqueous-phase processing must have occurred in clouds. This study presents the detailed molecular characterization of atmospheric organic matter (AOM) in supercooled cloud water collected at the Storm Peak Laboratory in the Rocky Mountains.
